abstract: With the rapid advancement of video understanding, existing benchmarks are becoming increasingly saturated, exposing a critical discrepancy between inflated leaderboard scores and real-world model capabilities. To address this widening gap, we introduce Video-MME-v2, a comprehensive benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate the robustness and faithfulness of video understanding. To systematically evaluate model capabilities, we design a \textbf{progressive tri-level hierarchy} that incrementally increases the complexity of video comprehension, ranging from multi-point visual information aggregation, to temporal dynamics modeling, and ultimately to complex multimodal reasoning. Besides, in contrast to conventional per-question accuracy, we propose a \textbf{group-based non-linear evaluation} strategy that enforces both consistency across related queries and coherence in multi-step reasoning. It penalizes fragmented or guess-based correctness and assigns credit only to answers supported by valid reasoning. To guarantee data quality, Video-MME-v2 is constructed through a rigorously controlled human annotation pipeline, involving 12 annotators and 50 independent reviewers. Backed by \textbf{3,300 human-hours} and up to \textbf{5 rounds} of quality assurance, Video-MME-v2 aims to serve as one of the most authoritative video benchmarks. Extensive experiments reveal a substantial gap between current best model Gemini-3-Pro and human experts, and uncover a clear hierarchical bottleneck where errors in visual information aggregation and temporal modeling propagate to limit high-level reasoning. We further find that thinking-based reasoning is highly dependent on textual cues, improving performance with subtitles but sometimes degrading it in purely visual settings. By exposing these limitations, Video-MME-v2 establishes a demanding new testbed for the development of next-generation video MLLMs.

citations.fullCitation: Chaoyou Fu, Yuhan Dai, Yongdong Luo, Lei Li, Shuhuai Ren, Renrui Zhang, Zihan Wang, Chenyu Zhou, Yunhang Shen, Mengdan Zhang, et al. Video-mme: The first-ever comprehensive evaluation benchmark of multi-modal llms in video analysis. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF conference on computer vision and pattern recognition, pages 24108–24118, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper introduces the original Video-MME benchmark, which is the direct predecessor to Video-MME-v2. The current work is explicitly framed as the next stage in the comprehensive evaluation of video MLLMs, building directly upon the foundation and goals established by this citation.
citations.title: Video-mme: The first-ever comprehensive evaluation benchmark of multi-modal llms in video analysis

citations.fullCitation: Chaoyou Fu, Peixian Chen, Yunhang Shen, Yulei Qin, Mengdan Zhang, Xu Lin, Jinrui Yang, Xiawu Zheng, Ke Li, Xing Sun, et al. Mme: A comprehensive evaluation benchmark for multimodal large language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2306.13394, 2023.
citations.justification: This citation is fundamentally important as it introduced the group-based question answering and non-linear scoring approach in the image domain. The Video-MME-v2 paper explicitly states it extends this core methodological concept to the video domain to assess consistency and coherence, making it a foundational reference for its novel evaluation strategy.
citations.title: Mme: A comprehensive evaluation benchmark for multimodal large language models

citations.fullCitation: Kunchang Li, Yali Wang, Yinan He, Yizhuo Li, Yi Wang, Yi Liu, Zun Wang, Jilan Xu, Guo Chen, Ping Luo, et al. Mvbench: A comprehensive multi-modal video understanding benchmark. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, pages 22195–22206, 2024.
citations.justification: MVBench is frequently cited as a prominent existing benchmark for video understanding. The authors of Video-MME-v2 position their work as a necessary advancement designed to address the gaps and limitations of prior benchmarks like MVBench, particularly in providing a more holistic hierarchy and robust evaluation metric.
citations.title: Mvbench: A comprehensive multi-modal video understanding benchmark

citations.fullCitation: Kairui Hu, Penghao Wu, Fanyi Pu, Wang Xiao, Yuanhan Zhang, Xiang Yue, Bo Li, and Ziwei Liu. Video-mmmu: Evaluating knowledge acquisition from multi-discipline professional videos. arXiv preprint arXiv:2501.13826, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is referenced as part of an emerging trend in benchmarks that focus on complex video reasoning. Video-MME-v2's design, especially its Level 3 tasks, directly engages with and aims to advance the evaluation of such sophisticated reasoning capabilities, making Video-MMMU a relevant contemporary work that the new benchmark is contextualized against.
citations.title: Video-mmmu: Evaluating knowledge acquisition from multi-discipline professional videos

intermediateReport:
### Technical Report: Video-MME-v2: Towards the Next Stage in Benchmarks for Comprehensive Video Understanding

This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "Video-MME-v2: Towards the Next Stage in Benchmarks for Comprehensive Video Understanding," focusing on its context, objectives, methodology, findings, and implications.

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The paper is presented by the "Video-MME Team." The project lead is identified as Chaoyou Fu (bradyfu24@gmail.com). Core contributors with equal contributions include Chaoyou Fu, Haozhi Yuan, Yuhao Dong, and Yi-Fan Zhang, among others. Senior leads are Xing Sun, Caifeng Shan, and Ran He. While specific institutional affiliations are not explicitly listed for all individuals in the provided text, the collaborative nature of the "Video-MME Team" suggests contributions from multiple researchers within the broader scientific community, often affiliated with various academic or industrial research institutions based on the typical composition of such teams in the field.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The field of video understanding, particularly with multimodal large language models (MLLMs), has experienced rapid advancements, transitioning from simple video comprehension to more complex reasoning tasks. Existing benchmarks for evaluating these models are described as increasingly saturated, exhibiting a discrepancy between reported leaderboard scores and actual real-world model capabilities.

Prior evaluation efforts in video MLLMs can be broadly categorized. Some benchmarks, such as MVBench and MotionBench, focus on domain-specific capabilities like fine-grained action understanding, while others like LongVideoBench and LVBench target long video comprehension. General video understanding benchmarks, including the original Video-MME, aim for comprehensive but often more basic evaluations. More recent works like VideoMMMU, MMVU, and VideoReasonBench have begun to address complex video reasoning.

However, the authors identify several limitations in these existing benchmarks:
*   **Lack of Comprehensive Evaluation Hierarchy:** Many benchmarks treat different capabilities as isolated aspects, lacking a structured taxonomy for holistic assessment of progressive complexity.
*   **Emphasis on Per-Question Accuracy:** Evaluations often rely solely on individual question correctness, which can overlook the need for consistent and trustworthy video comprehension. This approach may allow models to achieve high scores through fragmented or guess-based success rather than genuine multi-step reasoning.
*   **Limited Robustness and Faithfulness Assessment:** There is a perceived gap in rigorously evaluating the robustness of model understanding across related queries and the faithfulness of their reasoning processes.

Video-MME-v2 positions itself as an effort to address these identified gaps. It aims to provide a more rigorous and comprehensive evaluation standard by introducing a multi-level evaluation hierarchy and a group-based evaluation strategy. This approach seeks to move beyond isolated task performance to assess a model’s ability to achieve robust and faithful comprehension in dynamic, complex video scenarios, thus reflecting true understanding and reasoning capabilities more accurately than previous benchmarks.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop and introduce **Video-MME-v2**, a comprehensive benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate the robustness and faithfulness of video understanding in multimodal large language models (MLLMs). This objective is driven by a recognized critical discrepancy between inflated leaderboard scores on existing benchmarks and the actual real-world capabilities of these models.

The specific motivations and detailed objectives guiding the development of Video-MME-v2 include:

*   **Addressing Benchmark Saturation and Overestimation of Capabilities:** Existing benchmarks are increasingly saturated, leading to models achieving high scores that may not accurately reflect their genuine understanding. A key motivation is to provide a more challenging and discerning testbed that can reveal the true limitations of current video MLLMs.

*   **Systematic Evaluation of Model Capabilities through a Progressive Hierarchy:** The authors aim to overcome the limitations of benchmarks that lack a comprehensive evaluation hierarchy. Video-MME-v2 introduces a progressive tri-level hierarchy that incrementally increases the complexity of video comprehension tasks. This structured approach allows for systematic assessment, ranging from foundational visual information aggregation (Level 1), to dynamic temporal modeling (Level 2), and culminating in complex multimodal reasoning (Level 3). The motivation is to ensure a holistic evaluation that progresses from basic perception to sophisticated, human-like video comprehension.

*   **Enforcing Consistency and Coherence through a Group-Based Non-Linear Evaluation Strategy:** Traditional per-question accuracy is identified as insufficient for robust evaluation. The project is motivated to introduce a novel evaluation strategy that assesses both consistency across related queries and coherence in multi-step reasoning. This group-based non-linear scoring method is designed to penalize fragmented or guess-based correctness and to award credit only for answers supported by valid reasoning. This approach aims to provide a more faithful measure of genuine multimodal video proficiency.

*   **Guaranteeing Data Quality and Mitigating Data Contamination:** The reliability of a benchmark is heavily dependent on the quality of its data. A significant motivation is to construct Video-MME-v2 through a meticulously controlled human annotation pipeline. This includes substantial human involvement (12 annotators, 50 reviewers, 3,300 human-hours, up to 5 rounds of quality assurance), strict video selection criteria (recency-oriented to mitigate pretraining leakage, view-count thresholding, manual decontamination), and rigorous question design (group-based construction, adversarial options, verification with frontier models). The objective is to create an authoritative video benchmark with high-quality data.

*   **Exposing Current Model Limitations and Guiding Future Research:** By introducing a demanding testbed and a more rigorous evaluation methodology, the project aims to expose the specific limitations of current state-of-the-art video MLLMs. This includes uncovering hierarchical bottlenecks where errors at lower perception levels propagate to hinder high-level reasoning, and analyzing the impact of textual cues and thinking modes. The motivation is to provide actionable insights that can guide the development of next-generation video MLLMs towards more robust and faithful understanding.

In summary, Video-MME-v2 is motivated by the need for a more comprehensive, challenging, and reliable benchmark to accurately assess the evolving capabilities of video MLLMs and to foster progress in the field by highlighting areas requiring further development.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology of Video-MME-v2 encompasses a multi-faceted approach to benchmark design, dataset construction, and evaluation metrics, all aimed at comprehensive and rigorous assessment of video MLLMs.

**4.1. Benchmark Design**
The benchmark is structured around a novel evaluation framework comprising a progressive capability hierarchy and a group-based evaluation strategy, complemented by specific metrics.

*   **Progressive Capability Hierarchy:** This hierarchy categorizes video understanding skills into three levels of increasing complexity:
    *   **Level 1: Visual Information Aggregation:** Focuses on foundational perception and integration of information at specific timestamps. Sub-aspects include Visual Recognition (objects, attributes, scenes, OCR), Cross-Modal Consistency (visual-audio alignment), and Basic Counting & Calculation.
    *   **Level 2: Temporal Dynamics:** Builds on Level 1 by emphasizing the temporal evolution of events. Sub-aspects include Action & Motion Analysis (fine-grained action, motion trajectory, properties), Sequential Ordering (object/event order), and Causal Reasoning (cause-effect relations, future prediction).
    *   **Level 3: Complex Reasoning:** Simulates advanced, real-world cognitive tasks requiring professional knowledge and multi-hop inference. Sub-aspects include Narrative Understanding (plot analysis, symbolism), Social Dynamics (individual cognition, dyadic/collective interactions), and Physical World Reasoning (entity persistence, spatial understanding, counterfactual reasoning, counterintuitive comprehension).
    Questions are evenly distributed across these levels, designed to assess a holistic range of skills from basic visual perception to sophisticated, human-like video comprehension.

*   **Group Type Definition:** This strategy moves beyond per-question accuracy by explicitly modeling relationships among related queries:
    *   **Consistency-Based Group:** Aims to evaluate the breadth and granularity of a specific fundamental perception skill. Questions within a group vary in aspect (e.g., relative position vs. spatial reconstruction for spatial understanding) and granularity (e.g., object counting vs. motion counting for video-based counting). This provides a multi-granularity assessment within a domain.
    *   **Coherence-Based Group:** Designed to evaluate the depth of a model’s reasoning ability in complex tasks. Questions are structured as a logical progression a human would follow, tracing intermediate reasoning steps (e.g., clue localization, anomaly verification, purpose explanation, conclusion). This establishes an explicit reasoning chain to distinguish genuine multi-step reasoning from chance guesses.

*   **Metrics:**
    *   **Average Accuracy (Avg Acc):** The conventional mean of per-question correctness, provided for reference.
    *   **Group-level Non-linear Score:** The primary metric, designed to emphasize capability consistency and reasoning coherence.
        *   For **consistency groups**, a quadratic scoring function `(N/4)^2` is applied, where N is the number of correct answers out of 4 related questions. This penalizes isolated correct guesses and rewards consistent performance.
        *   For **coherence groups**, a first-error truncation mechanism is used. Only the longest consecutive sequence of correct answers from the first reasoning step counts. Any error truncates subsequent credit, ensuring only faithful reasoning chains are rewarded.

**4.2. Dataset Construction and Annotation**
The dataset comprises 800 videos, each paired with 4 questions and 8 answer options per question, totaling 3,200 questions. Its construction involved a rigorous human annotation and quality control process:

*   **Human Involvement:** Over 3,300 human-hours contributed by 12 annotators and 50 independent reviewers, with up to 5 rounds of quality assurance.
*   **Video Curation:**
    *   **Recency-Oriented Collection:** To mitigate data contamination, over 80% of videos were published in 2025 or later, with nearly 40% after October 2025, ensuring novelty to pre-training corpora.
    *   **Diversity:** Videos are sourced from a two-level taxonomy with four top-level domains (Sports & Competition, Lifestyle & Entertainment, Art & Literature, Knowledge & Education) and 31 subcategories, balancing subject matter and visual styles.
    *   **Quality Control:** Videos were filtered based on view counts (mean 4.83M, median 355K), with 84.3% exceeding 10,000 views, to ensure high-quality content.
    *   **Manual Decontamination:** Excluded classic films, TV works, and flagship influencer content to prevent memorization effects.
    *   **Length Distribution:** Questions progressively increase in average length from Q1 to Q4, aligning with increased logical depth. Answer options maintain consistent word counts to prevent length bias.
*   **Question and Option Design:**
    *   **Group-Based Construction:** Designed questions to inherently create logical chains for consistency and coherence.
    *   **Rigorous Verification:** Used frontier models (e.g., Gemini-3-Pro) for real-time validation and adversarial stress-testing to refine questions, detect language-only shortcuts, and strengthen distractors.
    *   **Low-Guessing Baseline:** An 8-option multiple-choice format reduces random guessing probability to 12.5%.
    *   **Strong Distractor Design:** Included at least one adversarial distractor per question, meticulously crafted to be plausible yet contradictory to fine-grained ground truth details, testing discriminative capabilities.
*   **Quality Assurance:**
    *   **Independent Quality-Control Team:** 50 reviewers minimized subjective bias, reviewing each sample at least twice and independently calculating human accuracy.
    *   **Text-Only Baseline Testing:** Gemini-3-Pro in text-only mode identified and removed questions solvable without visual information, ensuring multimodal dependence.
    *   **Multi-Round Cross-Validation:** Each question underwent three rounds of peer review by different annotators to eliminate ambiguity and fine-tune distractors.
    *   **Independent Blind Testing:** External evaluators performed multiple rounds of fine-grained checks to ensure generalizability and clarity.
    *   **Closed-Loop Iterative Verification:** Modified questions were re-subjected to baseline and blind testing, ensuring quality improvements.

**4.3. Experimental Setup**
Evaluations were conducted across proprietary (Gemini-3-Pro, GPT-5, Seed-2.0, MiMo-v2-Omni, Kimi-K2.5) and open-source models (Qwen, InternVL, LLaVA-Video). Two input settings were used: Visual Frames only (wo sub) and Visual Frames + Subtitles/Audio (w. sub). Temporal sampling rates adhered to model limitations (e.g., 1fps for native video, 64/512 frames for others). Omni-modal models (MiMo-v2-Omni, Gemini-3-Pro) processed raw audio in the 'w. sub' setting.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments conducted using Video-MME-v2 yield several findings regarding the current state and limitations of video MLLMs:

**5.1. Benchmark Results**

*   **Significant Gap with Human Performance:** Human experts achieved a Non-Lin Score of 90.7% and an average accuracy of 94.9%. In contrast, the best-performing commercial model, Gemini-3-Pro, attained a Non-Lin Score of 49.4%. This 41.3-point difference indicates a substantial gap between current MLLMs and human-level reliability and coherent reasoning in complex, long-horizon video understanding.
*   **Hierarchical Bottlenecks in Video Understanding:** Across all evaluated models, a monotonic performance degradation was observed from Level 1 (information aggregation) to Level 2 (temporal dynamics), and then to Level 3 (complex reasoning). This suggests that failures in high-level reasoning are not solely due to reasoning deficiencies but are also constrained by accumulated errors in foundational visual perception and temporal modeling. Improvements are posited to require holistic enhancement across the capability stack.
*   **Commercialization Model Dominance:** Proprietary models consistently outperformed open-source models. Gemini-3-Pro achieved the highest Non-Lin Score of 49.4 (w. sub), significantly higher than the best open-source model, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-Think, which scored 39.1. Commercial models also demonstrated greater robustness in the "visual frames only" setting (wo sub), suggesting less dependence on textual or auxiliary multimodal cues compared to many open-source counterparts.
*   **Benefit of Native Audio in Omni Architectures:** Models capable of processing raw audio (e.g., MiMo-v2-Omni, Gemini-3-Pro) showed notable performance gains when audio was provided (e.g., Gemini-3-Pro: +11.2 points, MiMo-v2-Omni: +8.7 points in Non-Lin Score with audio). This indicates that native audio integration provides complementary semantic and paralinguistic information, reducing reliance on purely textual subtitle/ASR pipelines.
*   **Performance of Small-Scale Models:** Model scale was not the sole determinant of performance. Qwen3.5-27B-Think achieved a Non-Lin Score of 31.4, outperforming several larger models and demonstrating that effective training recipes, data curation, long-context support, and alignment techniques can allow smaller models to remain competitive.

**5.2. Analysis Experiments**

*   **Advantage of Group-Based Nonlinear Scoring:** The Non-Lin Score consistently revealed lower performance than traditional Average Accuracy (e.g., Gemini-3-Pro: 66.1% Avg Acc vs. 49.4% Non-Lin Score). This discrepancy highlights that even state-of-the-art models often fail to consistently answer all correlated questions within a group, indicating a lack of robustness and faithfulness that is not captured by per-question metrics. The "Robustness Ratio" (Non-Lin Score / Avg Acc) served as an indicator of consistency, with stronger models exhibiting higher ratios (e.g., Gemini-3-Pro ~75%, LLaVA-Video-7B ~40%).
*   **Capability Consistency and Reasoning Coherence:**
    *   In **capability consistency groups**, model accuracies remained broadly comparable across Q1–Q4, suggesting a balanced difficulty for assessing specific skills.
    *   In **reasoning coherence groups**, all models showed a monotonic decline in accuracy from Q1 to Q4, reflecting the designed progression of logical dependency and difficulty. Stronger models exhibited a smoother decline, while weaker models showed more irregular patterns, possibly due to higher stochasticity.
    *   Mean-variance analysis confirmed that Gemini-3-Pro achieved both the highest mean accuracy and the lowest variance in capability consistency, indicating superior overall performance and stability.
*   **Effect of Thinking Mode:** Enabling a "Thinking mode" generally yielded larger performance gains in the "with subtitles" setting compared to "without subtitles." This suggests that explicit textual semantics provide anchors that amplify the effectiveness of reasoning mechanisms. However, for certain models (e.g., KimiVL-16B), enabling Thinking mode resulted in performance regressions, particularly in the "without subtitles" setting or at Level 3. This indicates that current reasoning mechanisms can introduce noise or an over-reliance on language priors when textual cues are absent.
*   **Overall Model Capability Analysis:**
    *   Models with a more complete profile of core capabilities (C1: Omni-modal information aggregation, C2: long-range temporal modeling, C3: complex reasoning) generally achieved higher scores.
    *   Parameter scale played a compensatory role; larger models (e.g., Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-Think at 512 frames) could partly offset an incomplete capability profile, sometimes outperforming models with more explicit capability coverage but smaller scales.
    *   Increased frame count (long-context processing) significantly improved performance for the same model (e.g., Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-Think gained 8.5 points when switching from 64 to 512 frames).
*   **Capability Radar Analysis:** A radar chart comparison revealed that Gemini-3-Pro excelled in "Frames&Audio" and "long-horizon temporal reasoning" dimensions. Despite these strengths, all models, including the state-of-the-art, showed significant limitations in challenging dimensions such as "Action & Motion" and "Physical World Reasoning," with scores often remaining below 30.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

Video-MME-v2 offers several significant contributions and potential impacts on the research and development of video MLLMs:

*   **Advanced Evaluation Framework:** The benchmark introduces a progressive tri-level capability hierarchy and a group-based evaluation strategy. This moves beyond traditional per-question accuracy to provide a more comprehensive and nuanced assessment of model capabilities, including robustness, consistency across related queries, and coherence in multi-step reasoning. This framework is significant for offering a finer-grained understanding of where models succeed and fail.

*   **Rigorously Curated High-Quality Dataset:** The meticulous human annotation pipeline, involving extensive human-hours, multi-round quality assurance, recency-oriented video collection, and adversarial question design, results in a high-quality dataset. This ensures the benchmark's integrity by mitigating risks of data contamination (pre-training leakage) and by providing questions that genuinely require multimodal understanding rather than text-only shortcuts.

*   **Exposure of Current Model Limitations:** The experiments reveal a substantial performance gap between frontier video MLLMs and human experts, highlighting that current models are far from achieving human-level video understanding. The findings also identify hierarchical bottlenecks, where errors at lower-level perceptual tasks propagate to limit higher-level reasoning. This diagnostic capability is critical for focusing future research efforts on specific weaknesses.

*   **Insights into Reasoning Mechanisms:** The analysis of "thinking modes" and the impact of textual cues provides insights into how current MLLMs leverage different modalities for reasoning. The observation that thinking modes can improve performance with subtitles but sometimes degrade it without textual cues suggests an over-reliance on language priors and indicates areas for improving purely visual reasoning capabilities.

*   **Guidance for Next-Generation Model Development:** By systematically breaking down capabilities, revealing hierarchical dependencies, and quantifying the impact of factors like native audio integration and long-context processing, Video-MME-v2 provides actionable guidance for researchers. It encourages the development of models with stronger omni-modal perception, more robust temporal modeling, and more faithful, less stochastic reasoning mechanisms.

*   **Authoritative Benchmark Standard:** The comprehensive design, rigorous data quality, and detailed analysis position Video-MME-v2 as a demanding new testbed. It is intended to serve as one of the most authoritative video benchmarks, driving future development in the field by setting a higher standard for evaluating robust and faithful video understanding. Its continuous availability and project page further support its role as a resource for the research community.
overview:
## Redefining the Standard for Video Understanding

The rapid evolution of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has pushed the boundaries of video comprehension, moving from simple object recognition to complex, long-horizon reasoning. However, as models improve, existing benchmarks often become "saturated," showing high leaderboard scores that do not always translate to robust real-world performance. This discrepancy suggests that current evaluation methods may overstate model capabilities by relying on isolated, per-question accuracy that can be inflated through lucky guesses or language-based shortcuts.

![Video-MME-v2 Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05015v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: The Video-MME-v2 framework features a tri-level capability hierarchy and a group-based non-linear evaluation strategy to assess robustness and faithfulness in video understanding.*

Video-MME-v2 is a comprehensive benchmark designed to address these limitations. It moves away from fragmented testing by introducing a structured taxonomy of video understanding and a rigorous evaluation strategy that rewards consistency and logical coherence. By shifting the focus from whether a model can answer a single question to whether it truly understands the underlying dynamics of a video across related queries, the benchmark provides a more faithful measure of genuine multimodal proficiency.

## A Progressive Hierarchy of Video Capabilities

To systematically evaluate the depth of video understanding, Video-MME-v2 establishes a tri-level hierarchy that categorizes capabilities based on increasing cognitive complexity. This structure ensures that models are tested not just on what they see, but on how they integrate temporal information and apply high-level reasoning.

**Level 1: Visual Information Aggregation**
At the foundational level, the benchmark assesses the model's ability to perceive and integrate information at specific moments. This includes Visual Recognition (identifying objects, scenes, and text), Cross-Modal Consistency (aligning visual and audio cues), and Basic Counting and Calculation. These tasks form the "perceptual base" upon which more complex reasoning is built.

**Level 2: Temporal Dynamics**
The second level focuses on the evolution of events over time. It requires models to understand Action and Motion Analysis (fine-grained trajectories and properties), Sequential Ordering (the chronological flow of events), and Causal Reasoning (predicting future outcomes or identifying the cause of an observed event). This level tests the model’s ability to treat video as a continuous temporal stream rather than a collection of static frames.

**Level 3: Complex Reasoning**
The final level simulates advanced cognitive tasks. It includes Narrative Understanding (deconstructing plots and metaphors), Social Behavior Analysis (understanding individual and collective interactions), and Physical World Reasoning (tracking entity persistence and performing counterfactual analysis). These tasks often require professional-grade knowledge and multi-hop inference, reflecting the most challenging aspects of human-like video comprehension.

## Group-Based Evaluation for Consistency and Coherence

A core contribution of Video-MME-v2 is its move beyond traditional accuracy metrics. In standard benchmarks, a model might answer a question correctly by chance or by exploiting textual biases. Video-MME-v2 mitigates this by organizing questions into "Groups" of four related queries per video, using two distinct strategies:

**Consistency-Based Groups**
These groups evaluate the breadth and stability of a specific skill. For instance, a model might be asked four different questions about spatial relationships in a scene. To reward consistent performance and penalize fragmented success, the benchmark uses a quadratic scoring function:

$$S = \left(\frac{N}{4}\right)^2$$

In this formula, $N$ represents the number of correct answers within the group of four. Under this scoring system, answering only one or two questions correctly yields a disproportionately low score, while answering all four correctly is required to achieve the full credit. This encourages models to demonstrate a robust grasp of the underlying capability rather than a surface-level association.

**Coherence-Based Groups**
These groups model a logical reasoning chain. Questions are structured to follow a human-like progression, starting from localized clues (e.g., "What object is out of place?") and leading to high-level conclusions (e.g., "Why did the character react that way?"). To ensure faithfulness, these groups use a first-error truncation mechanism. If a model fails at a foundational reasoning step, such as $Q_1$ or $Q_2$, it receives no credit for subsequent correct answers in that group. This ensures that a correct conclusion ($Q_4$) is only rewarded if it is supported by a valid and coherent reasoning process.

## Rigorous Data Construction and Quality Control

The reliability of any benchmark depends on its data quality and its resistance to "data contamination"—where models might have seen the test data during their pre-training phase. Video-MME-v2 implements several rigorous measures to ensure the integrity of its evaluation.

![Video Categories](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05015v1/video_category.png)
*Figure 2: The benchmark spans a diverse taxonomy of 31 subcategories across sports, lifestyle, art, and knowledge domains.*

To prevent memorization, the team utilized a recency-oriented collection strategy. More than $80\%$ of the videos in the dataset were published in $2025$ or later, with nearly $40\%$ published after October $2025$. This ensures the content is novel even to the most recently trained models. Furthermore, the team manually excluded famous films, TV shows, and viral influencer content that might be present in large-scale web-crawled training sets.

![Publish Date Stats](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05015v1/video_publish_month.png)
*Figure 3: Distribution of video publication dates, highlighting the emphasis on recent content to mitigate data leakage.*

The annotation process involved over $3,300$ human-hours, with $12$ primary annotators and $50$ independent reviewers. Each question was refined through five rounds of quality assurance. To increase the difficulty and reduce random success, each question is presented in an 8-option multiple-choice format, reducing the random guessing probability to just $12.5\%$. Annotators also meticulously crafted adversarial distractors—options that appear plausible but are contradicted by fine-grained details in the video—to test the models' discriminative capabilities.

## Benchmarking the State-of-the-Art

The evaluation of prominent models, including Gemini-3-Pro, GPT-5, and open-source models like Qwen3.5, reveals a significant gap between current AI performance and human experts. While human experts achieved a non-linear score of $90.7\%$ and an average accuracy of $94.9\%$, the highest-performing commercial model, Gemini-3-Pro, reached only $49.4$ in its non-linear score.

![Model Accuracy Q1-Q4](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05015v1/q1-q4_models_acc_0406.png)
*Figure 4: Performance trends across question groups. Coherence groups (middle) show a monotonic decline, reflecting the increasing logical depth required for later questions.*

A key finding is the presence of "hierarchical bottlenecks." Across all models, performance steadily declines from Level 1 to Level 3. This suggests that errors in basic perception (Level 1) or temporal tracking (Level 2) propagate upward, preventing the model from succeeding in complex reasoning tasks. Proprietary models generally demonstrated more robustness than open-source counterparts, particularly when textual subtitles were removed, indicating a more sophisticated native visual understanding.

![Effect of Thinking Mode](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05015v1/scientific_think_effect_v2.png)
*Figure 5: The impact of enabling "Thinking Mode" across different levels. While often beneficial, it can lead to regressions in certain models when visual cues are absent.*

The study also analyzed the "Thinking Mode" (where models generate a chain of thought before answering). While this mode generally improves performance—especially when textual subtitles provide a semantic anchor—it can sometimes lead to regressions. For some models, explicit reasoning resulted in an over-reliance on linguistic priors, causing them to hallucinate or misinterpret purely visual information when textual cues were missing. This highlights that "more thinking" is not always better if the underlying visual-linguistic alignment is weak.

## Significance for Future Research

Video-MME-v2 establishes a more demanding and diagnostic testbed for the next generation of video MLLMs. By quantifying consistency and coherence, it exposes the stochastic nature of current models, where a "correct" answer is often not backed by a robust understanding of the video's context.

![Capability Radar](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05015v1/radar_second_head.png)
*Figure 6: A radar chart comparing model capabilities. Significant gaps remain in Action & Motion and Physical World Reasoning across all tested models.*

The benchmark's findings suggest several paths for future development. First, the integration of native audio processing (omni-modal architectures) provides a clear performance boost, as seen in models like Gemini-3-Pro and MiMo-v2-Omni. Second, increasing the frame sampling rate (long-context processing) remains a reliable way to improve temporal modeling. Finally, the $41.3$ point gap between the best AI and human performance in the non-linear metric indicates that achieving truly faithful, human-like video reasoning remains a major challenge. Video-MME-v2 provides the necessary granularity to track progress toward this goal, ensuring that future advancements are measured not just by accuracy, but by the reliability of the model's understanding.

keyInsights: A substantial performance gap exists between frontier MLLMs and human experts, with the best models achieving only about half of human reliability and coherent reasoning in complex video understanding.
Model failures in high-level reasoning are often due to hierarchical bottlenecks, where accumulated errors from lower-level visual perception and temporal modeling propagate upwards.
The group-based non-linear scoring method is crucial for revealing a lack of robustness and faithfulness in MLLMs that traditional average accuracy metrics fail to capture.
originalProblem: Existing video understanding benchmarks for MLLMs are saturated, leading to an overestimation of model capabilities and a discrepancy between reported scores and real-world performance.
Current evaluation frameworks often lack a comprehensive, structured hierarchy for holistic assessment, treating different capabilities as isolated aspects.
Traditional per-question accuracy metrics are insufficient for robust evaluation, overlooking the need for consistent comprehension and coherent multi-step reasoning.
results: Human experts achieved a Non-Lin Score of 90.7%, while the best commercial model, Gemini-3-Pro, scored 49.4%, indicating a 41.3-point gap in reliable and coherent video understanding.
All evaluated models showed monotonic performance degradation from Level 1 (visual aggregation) to Level 3 (complex reasoning), highlighting the presence of hierarchical bottlenecks.
Native audio integration in omni-modal architectures provided significant performance gains (e.g., Gemini-3-Pro improved by 11.2 points in Non-Lin Score), underscoring its complementary value.
solution: Video-MME-v2 establishes a progressive tri-level capability hierarchy, assessing skills from foundational visual aggregation to temporal dynamics and complex multimodal reasoning.
It implements a novel group-based non-linear evaluation strategy that penalizes fragmented correctness and rewards consistent performance and faithful reasoning chains.

The benchmark dataset was meticulously constructed using a rigorous human annotation pipeline, including recency-oriented video collection and adversarial question design to ensure high quality and mitigate data contamination.
summary.summary: Video-MME-v2 introduces a benchmark for evaluating the robustness and faithfulness of multimodal large language models in comprehensive video understanding. It proposes a progressive tri-level evaluation hierarchy and a group-based non-linear scoring mechanism, revealing a 41.3-point performance gap between human experts and the best MLLMs, and identifying hierarchical bottlenecks in current models.

title: Video-MME-v2: Towards the Next Stage in Benchmarks for Comprehensive Video Understanding
